other_observers: On day one, just Tom Walters, Michelle Walters, and Meghan Walters(original finder). On day two, Tom Walters, Michelle Walters, Meghan Walters, Cole Wild, Nick Komar, Rachel Hopper, and Cade Cropper. On day three, Tom Walters, and Michelle Walters. I saw the bird on all of these days as well. On September 15, 2012, individuals that saw the bird were Steve Stachowiak, Denise Bretting, Meghan Walters, Tom Walters, and myself.; My sister actually pointed the bird out to me as we were sitting at our table talking about the lack of hummingbirds seen the last two weeks. I then got our camera, took some photos, and compared them to the descriptions in the Sibley guide. I was not confident as to exactly what it was but was sure that it was from the genus Archilochus, so I sent photos to Bill Schmoker who responded that it did look like it might be a Ruby-throated Hummingbird. He did say though that if I could get even bette
duration: It was seen off and on until 6:00 p.m.
description: The bill of the hummingbird was all black. Even though it is a subjective feature, the bill appeared shorter and much straighter than a Black-Chinned Hummingbird's bill. The feathers at the base of its bill ,on the top side were slightly grayish, while from just above that (there is almost no way for me to accurately describe the placement of that transition point) on up the head and over the crown were green. From there on down the back was about the same shade of darker green except for a few lime colored feathers mixed in. The wings were black, with the last primary being pointed and somewhat narrower than the primary just before it. The primary projection was not quite to the end of the tail. The top of the tail was green all the way to where it turned black with white spots at the end. In photos taken by someone else the tail feathers on this bird were more rounded than sharp. The under tail coverts were bright white. The belly and sides were washed with a slight gray. The throat was bright white with no feathers of any other color other than a few that had small amounts of gray on their tips. The eye was black and there was a white spot behind the eye, not around or completely over top the eye. Bordering the throat and extending down from the eye was a gray area almost like a stripe. In front of the eye and extending to the bill was a very dark, almost black stripe. In general the contrast of the dark head and the brilliant white throat was what first caught me off guard (besides the fact that we have not had any hummingbirds at our feeder for two weeks).Behaviors: It fed at our feeders or just sat in the tree and made ocasional chattering. While it was at our feeder and while it was flying it never pumped its tail. I did video tape it several times at our feeder to support that feature. It seemed quite cold the second morning it was here. It would try and sit in the direct sunlight and it would frequently strech its wings from side to side and fan its tail. It looked really funny when it would do this.Call: A chattering. I did manage to tape it if that would be any help. I don't know how to distinguish between Ruby-throated and Black-chinned calls though.Plumage: Adult
photographer: Photographer: Sean Walters, Sean Walters, Sean Walters, Sean Walters
